chapters.title: Leading through the crisis
chapters.text:
Putting together a strong supply chain response during a pandemic is hard. We are taught to be ready for the worst, but once a crisis lands, the plan often falls short. Still, there are ways to act, even when you are not fully ready.

Start with your people. Put your staff first. If you do not already have a way to reach them, set one up. Think about changing how work gets done.

Lean on healthy doubt. A cool head and good judgment matter for anyone trying to make sense of a messy situation. As the story unfolds, new facts come out, so even official sources may carry mixed messages.

Local contacts can share useful signals. Keep those ties strong.

Run outage drills to map out surprise scenarios. Picture and act out how your company would work under pressure from unlikely angles. It may cost time and money, but the lessons can leave your business stronger.

Build a core emergency operations center, or EOC. Most firms have something like this, but often only at the corporate or business unit level. A wider EOC that reaches every level of the company is needed.

Few businesses stand on their own. Most lean on stakeholders, customers, and supply chains. To handle the supply chain impact during a crisis, understand your full supply network, including suppliers further up the chain, so you can size up likely impacts when a crisis hits. Know your weak spots and pool the risk. Drop supply chains that put your business at risk. Write business continuity plans that cover transport, communication, supply, and cash flow. Take care of your people, since they also need a backup plan. Plan for more automation and flexible work to handle staffing gaps.

When you build a continuity plan, include not just key stakeholders but also suppliers and customers.

Reshape your supply chain. Use dual sourcing to lower the risk of disruption. Move to local sourcing so production sits closer to local suppliers. That path has its own risks, but with sources spread out, transport costs tend to drop.

chapters.title: Business is changing. Will you adapt or fall behind
chapters.text:
The Covid 19 crisis is still taking lives every day and pushing public health systems to the limit. Vaccines are rolling out, and the picture should improve, but the timeline is still unclear. The pandemic has hit every part of life, and the economy is one of them. Stronger economies absorb the blow, while weaker ones shrink. No matter the size, businesses are facing the need to change or close up shop.

The Harvard Business Review has laid out a set of guidelines for responding to fast moving events, communicating, and pulling out the lessons that matter.

Collect the freshest data. Things are moving faster than ever. At first, the virus looked like China's problem, and many thought China had it under control. Today, a new strain is in play, and different countries keep taking turns leading the grim numbers. Sharing updates each day is key to staying relevant and keeping a hold on your situation in the bigger picture.

Update your view of the world every day, not just to refresh the numbers, but to reshape how you see things.

Take hype in stride. Information changes fast. We tend to brush off soft signals and over react to loud ones. We need to take a steadier view. Whatever it is, we tend to make it bigger than it is. Before you act on a piece of news, check how reliable the source is.

Information alone does not raise awareness. Today, we have more information than ever. Many employers think that is enough for their staff, but companies need to share the right facts and the meaning behind them, or people will read it the wrong way, get confused, and waste time in pointless debate.

Pick your experts and forecasts with care. Epidemiology, virology, public health, and other fields are central to clearing up complex information. Still, experts often disagree on the big questions, and that is because we are dealing with cases no one has seen before. It always helps to look at more than one source, and every reliable source should be guided by expert view.

Stay flexible about the state of things. Large businesses are rarely nimble, with many staff and a thick layer of process. Managers often hold off on sharing plans until they are fully sure, and then they hesitate to change them for fear of looking unsure or stirring up trouble inside the firm.

Pay close attention to a few key areas. Communication, so policies reach people fast, clearly, and evenly. Employee needs, so that travel limits and health concerns are met, with a resource hub for the must have information. Travel, with clear rules on destinations, reasons, approvals, and updates. Remote work, with a clear plan for how people will work and when the plan will be reviewed. Supply chain stability, by building safety stocks and finding backup sources, working with suppliers on shared problems and interim plans.

chapters.title: Questions to ask as you respond to the crisis
chapters.text:
The coronavirus outbreak is a reminder for companies to take a hard look at their plans for staff, operations, and customer safety. In response to the spread, companies should sit with a few key questions.

How can we keep workers from being exposed on the job. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention advises staff to stay home when they have respiratory symptoms or a fever over 100.4 F, wash hands for at least 20 seconds or use hand sanitizer, leave work if symptoms show up, and cover coughs and sneezes with a tissue, elbow, or shoulder.

When should workers or visitors stay out of the workplace. Staff with coronavirus symptoms should not come in. Managers need to remember that the virus is highly contagious. They should send an employee showing signs of Covid 19 home right away. Visitors with symptoms or at high risk should be moved away from other staff, guided on how to leave the workplace, sent for a medical check, and kept apart from others.

If Covid 19 spreads out of control, companies should use thermal scanners to check temperatures and turn away anyone reading over 100.4 F.

Companies are advised to keep people out for 14 days if they have been exposed to the virus or have just come from a high risk area.

For meetings, cap the number of attendees and ask older staff or those with chronic conditions to join virtually, as the Harvard Business Review notes.

What will our benefits look like if employees work from home, or if we shut the site. With more people falling ill, the number of staff working from home is growing. Many companies have already updated their policies, while others are still working it out. Employers need to see that remote work can be done just as carefully as work in the office. Staff should not be punished for taking sick leave. Guaranteeing that builds trust and leads to honest self reporting, which cuts down on exposure.

Covid 19 spreads mostly through droplets from coughing and sneezing.

A side note. Around 60 percent of employers in a Harvard Business Review survey said they had set up the right conditions for staff to work from home, with 46 percent already doing so and 13 percent planning to.

chapters.title: Make your supply chain pandemic ready
chapters.text:
Putting together a strong supply chain response during a pandemic is hard. We are taught to be ready for the worst, but once a crisis lands, the plan often falls short. Still, there are ways to act, even when you are not fully ready.

Start with your people. Put your staff first. If you do not already have a way to reach them, set one up. Think about changing how work gets done.

chapters.title: Try these moves before laying people off
chapters.text:
During a hard stretch like the coronavirus outbreak, the urge to cut costs is understandable. Leaders have a duty to keep their businesses alive. They have to make the right call, and sometimes a hard one, to handle the damage from the disaster. If they pull it off, the company will come back stronger and create more value. So, before you start cutting and laying off, take a look at the moves below.

Communicate openly. In hard times, many leaders worry that if they admit the truth, their teams will see them as weak. Some even fear that staff will start looking for work elsewhere. They choose to act like everything is fine and that the hard truth has no impact on the business. That is probably the worst move a leadership team can make. Admitting that things are rough shows that you are a steady, strong leader. It also builds trust between the top team and the rest of the staff, with the message that whatever comes, they can carry it together.

Set clear goals that match the company's reality. Avoid empty lines you do not believe in. Speak from the heart, and your staff will feel respected and safe.

Share the pain. As we said, a leader has to be ready to make tough calls. If cuts are coming, include yourself. If you do not, your staff will be unhappy, and some will hold it against you. They will feel like prey, forced to give up everything while the CEO does not.

Talk with clients about keeping the work going. Not every project has to end. Some can just pause for a while.

Bring employees in to crowdsource ideas. Crowdsourcing, in company terms, means asking your staff for ideas. You may worry that people will pitch ideas that cannot be done. If you ignore them, the people who raised them will feel brushed off. The whole process can feel heavy. You may also fear that letting your team speak up will dent your image as a strong leader. These concerns are real, but the gains from this move outweigh them. When you ask your staff, you make them feel seen and important. The many minds, many ideas rule has a flip side. You may pick up valuable advice.

Look at every option. Before you push through layoffs, look at less obvious ways to cut costs. You can offer some staff a part time schedule, or unpaid leave, framed as a sabbatical. These moves are not fun, but they help people keep their jobs in hard times.

chapters.title: How to lead a remote team
chapters.text:
In response to the unease from Covid 19, many companies told their staff to work from home. For most of them, remote work was a brand new experience. Many daily routines had to be done a little differently. If only there had been time to design and test policies in advance. The pandemic did not wait, and there was no spare moment to pick the best ways to handle the new work setup. Managers have research based moves to lift the focus and output of remote staff. Still, remote work comes with real hurdles.

Lack of in person supervision. Many managers worry that their staff will slack off when no one is watching in person. The question of trust and personal responsibility comes up again. Trust and responsibility are a two way street between a manager and an employee.

Remote workers who have never met in person are less likely to extend understanding in stressful moments.

Lack of access to information. Getting information from coworkers can take longer than usual. In an office, we are usually less distracted and can focus better. It just takes time to get used to a remote rhythm.

Social isolation. Many employees live alone. Being forced to work from home can lead to depression, since there is no face to face contact with other people.

Distractions at home. Employees with families or roommates have a different set of complaints. For some of them, those around them are a steady distraction.

Now, here are moves to support remote staff.

Set up structured daily check ins. Run daily calls with your remote staff. Choose one on one talks or group calls, depending on the team.

Offer a range of communication tools. Email alone is not enough. It helps to have richer tech for remote work. Video calls give people visual signals they would get face to face.

Set clear rules of engagement. Lay out a set of rules to organize remote work. Staff will find it clearer and be more productive when they have real guidelines. They will appreciate it if you set the means, the frequency, and the best time for engagement.

chapters.title: Communicate early and often through a crisis
chapters.text:
In unpredictable times, leaders face questions they cannot answer. Those in charge need to communicate fast throughout the crisis. Even if you are still trying to gauge the size of the problem, honesty and openness can help protect the trust you have built. Be understanding, because you and your teams are in it together.

Try to see the situation from your team's point of view and show empathy.

Set up one team for central communication. Decentralized communication can work in big, complex organizations, where small units act like startups inside a larger firm. Put together a crisis response team with key people from different departments. In an emergency, a focused team of five to seven is essential. Include a leader, a corporate communications person, an HR executive, and a specialist in the relevant field. The team should meet often to handle the situation as it changes, be the main source of crisis information, share updates on key developments, stay transparent by sharing what is known and what is not, and keep messages brief and to the point. Avoid long, complex messages from health experts or lawyers.

Studies show that leaders play a special role in cutting employee anxiety, as the Harvard Business Review notes.

Keep employees in the loop. Staff turn ideas into action and act as the face of the business. Without enough information about the company's direction in hard times, staff can send the wrong message to the outside world. The company should adopt a policy of sharing all available information with staff in a clear way.

To talk with employees, companies should post information often where everyone can see it, in person or online, lay out the decision making process for travel and remote work, not hold back on reaching out at least three times a week, and share updates as new information comes in.

Update customers on a steady rhythm. Customers need a custom approach to communication, focused on highlighting customer priorities, with a letter to grocery shoppers listing every safety measure put in place during the pandemic. Make room for relief where you can. Travel and flight companies can lean on this. Skip the sales pitch and lead with empathy.

chapters.title: Looking back
chapters.text:
Covid 19 has taken many lives and changed most of our daily habits beyond recognition. Many of us had no choice but to work from home. Some took it as a gift, with more time to spend with family. Others living alone found it tough, as their social life and in person contact faded.

These times are hard on everyone, with no exception. Still, we can work together to make each day a little easier on each other. In our work lives, we should say what we feel, what we fear, and what can be done to soften the hard conditions. As an employee, support your coworkers and chase company goals that may be shifting faster than usual. If you are a leader, be open, share the information on hand, and listen to your staff. Compassion and empathy matter more than chasing profit by any means.

The crisis can also bring out the best in people and open up new chances. We may have already learned to be more responsible and more respectful of each other. One of the biggest lessons for businesses is that being humane is good for your reputation and your returns. When your staff, partners, and customers feel that you put their needs first and take their worries seriously, they will do their best to back you in hard times and stay loyal for years to come.

A short exercise. In the middle of a crisis, we are all vulnerable. Beyond our work lives, we need to look after our minds and emotions. The authors of this book share a few ways to build resilience.

Calm your mind. When you quiet the loop of worst case scenarios, even for a moment, your chances of understanding what is going on go up. Try familiar practices like yoga and meditation. If those feel too advanced, start with slow breathing. Make your exhale at least two seconds longer than your inhale.

Distract yourself. When the feelings feel too much to handle, switch to an activity that needs little thought. You can look out the window and count the trees in your yard, finally clean your desk, or run out for a small item.

Support others. We are all in this together, and the bottom line is that we need to be kind and supportive. Being kind means being willing to make the first move. Be the first to reach out and build a real connection with someone who needs it.
